reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Conservative subsidiarity (parental choice and school autonomy), merit-based selection (talent development), and suspicion of state-administered equity proxies that risk eroding institutional independence and parental authority.
reactions.interest: Parents exercising educational choice for their children; selective schools preserving academic standards and institutional autonomy; merit-based advancement of talent regardless of background.
reactions.public_good_frame: A society advances talent most efficiently when schools are free to select for academic merit and when parents retain authority over their children's educational path. Equity is best served by removing barriers to application (information, cost), not by quota-based intake reweighting that displaces merit-based selection.
reactions.argument: We support the goal of wider access to selective schools among capable applicants across SES groups, but S1's mechanism raises three structural concerns: (1) Replacing the central exam with a 'less preparation-sensitive assessment' is coded as weaker selection, which risks selecting for coachability-resistance rather than actual talent—this inverts the meritocratic principle. A genuinely rigorous assessment is preparation-resistant by virtue of measuring deep understanding, not by deliberate design-fuzziness. (2) Regional equity quotas subordinate merit-based selection to demographic balancing; this is a distributive claim, not a selection claim, and converts the school into an instrument of state equity policy rather than a meritocratic institution. If the quota is binding (i.e., some quota-admitted students would not meet the academic threshold), the school ceases to select for talent and instead selects for demographic fit. (3) The SES indicator—flagged as unresolved, gameable, and requiring administrative scaffolding—transfers gatekeeping power from schools and parents to state bureaucracy. Even if well-intentioned, this erodes the institutional autonomy that allows schools to set and defend their own standards. We recognise that paid test prep creates an unfair advantage; the conservative response is to make the assessment itself resistant to coaching (i.e. genuinely rigorous and broad-based) and to ensure all families have access to factual information about what the assessment measures—not to obscure the assessment or replace merit with quotas. Non-state maintainers (church, foundation) should retain the right to set their own admission standards within their institutional mission; subsidy-conditional uniform rules are a form of state control over institutional conscience.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would support S1 if: (a) the new assessment is demonstrated through independent psychometric validation to measure academic talent more accurately than the current exam, not merely to resist coaching; (b) regional quotas are framed as a pilot affecting no more than 10-15% of intake, with rigorous evaluation against a control cohort before expansion, and only if the quota-admitted students meet the same academic threshold as merit-selected peers (i.e. the quota does not lower the bar, only reweight among qualified applicants); (c) the SES indicator is built and piloted in a way that does not expand state access to family financial/benefit data without explicit parental consent; (d) non-state maintainers retain a carve-out for their own institutional mission-based admissions, especially for faith-formation tracks.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Conservative opposition to state-imposed caps on parental choice; belief that talent development requires stratification by ability; concern that residual selective places will become even more skewed without admission reform; and skepticism of state-funded 'advanced programmes' in general schools as a substitute for genuine selectivity.
reactions.interest: Parents with high-achieving children seeking to preserve access to schools that group by ability; selective schools defending their academic model; families relying on early selection as a lock-in mechanism to secure a known educational pathway.
reactions.public_good_frame: Early academic tracking allows schools to concentrate their teaching on the developmental level of their cohort, raising standards for high-achievers and preventing boredom-driven disengagement. Caps on selective entry, absent admission reform, simply shrink the pool without improving equity—the residual places become even more skewed to high-SES families with better information and resources. A pre-announced cap also creates a 'last-chance' panic that harms transition cohorts most. The conservative approach is to preserve the selective model while reforming how students enter it.
reactions.argument: S2 has three fatal flaws from a conservative standpoint: (1) It assumes that caps on state schools will not trigger substitution to non-state maintainers; the evidence_status flags this as an assumption, not demonstrated fact. If caps tighten state-school selectivity while church and foundation schools expand, the effect is simply to relocate sorting and to advantage families wealthy enough to afford non-state fees. This mirrors the Polish political dynamic and is not a conservative endorsement. (2) The peer-composition and retention benefits rest on the 'asymmetric peer-effect' mechanism, which the scenario itself tags as contested and weak. Retaining high-achievers in general-school upper grades may not improve peer composition if those high-achievers are a minority; it may demoralize them instead. The scenario does not resolve this mechanism—it merely asserts that 'advanced programmes' will hold them. (3) Caps create a multi-decade transition where capped intake and grandfathered cohorts run in parallel, generating administrative complexity and fairness problems for transition cohorts. Transition families face shrinking odds of entry without the admission-rule change; S2 without S1 will mechanically skew residual intake even more to high-SES families. A society serious about meritocracy should reform entry rules (S1) before imposing caps. (4) The Polish precedent shows that caps are politically fragile; a successor government can reverse them with little cost. Unless legislated entrenchment (supermajority, multi-year notice) is included, S2 is a permanent vulnerability to policy whipsaw. The conservative response is: if you want to reduce early sorting, pair it with admission reform and entrenchment; do not impose caps alone, which achieve nothing except to shrink high-SES families' access while legitimising the state's right to ration educational opportunity.
reactions.condition_to_change: We could support a capped trajectory if: (a) it is paired with S1-type admission reform (less-coachable assessment, regional equity quotas for 10-15% of intake) so that residual seats do not become even more skewed; (b) non-state maintainers are legally bound to follow the same cap and admission rules via maintainer-agreement legislation, not merely Ministry negotiation, so that caps do not simply shift demand to unregulated schools; (c) an entrenchment mechanism (supermajority/multi-year notice requirement for reversal) is legislated alongside the cap, explicitly acknowledging the Polish risk; (d) 'advanced programmes' in general schools are piloted with rigorous peer-composition and outcome tracking before assuming they retain high-achievers; and (e) the transition is managed via a carefully calibrated grandfathering schedule that does not leave transition cohorts worse off than their predecessors.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Conservative belief in early identification and focused development of talent; concern that delay to age 14 masks sorting rather than eliminating it; skepticism of comprehensive schools' ability to maintain academic standards; and principled opposition to removing parental choice to select an early-track school for their child.
reactions.interest: Parents of high-achieving children; selective schools; families valuing academic rigor and focused peer groups; faith-based schools seeking to retain institutional identity; teachers in selective schools.
reactions.public_good_frame: A society that delays identifying and developing talent until age 14 misses the window for deepening mathematical and scientific foundations and for fostering intellectual ambition. Comprehensive schools serving mixed-ability cohorts cannot simultaneously challenge high-achievers and support struggling learners without either holding back the former or overwhelming the latter. The Finnish and Polish reforms show delayed selection can improve equity, but only with massive investment in teacher quality, diagnostic systems, and curriculum—costs the scenario flags as 'high' and 'unestimated'. Hungary's teacher shortage is already acute; comprehensive detracking without those investments will degrade quality across the system. The conservative response is to preserve early tracking while ensuring entry is fair and talent is identified early and intensively developed.
reactions.argument: S3 is the most ambitious and most dangerous scenario from a conservative standpoint for five reasons: (1) The mechanism relies on a peer-effect link that the scenario itself tags as 'contested' and 'weak.' The claim is that a weaker SES-outcome gradient emerges 'within a decade via broader access to high-achieving peers,' but this is neither proven for Hungary nor even established in the cross-country literature as a load-bearing channel. If the gradient does not narrow, the reform has achieved nothing except to destroy the selective schools that were at least delivering high academic standards for those admitted. (2) The scenario explicitly acknowledges that 'sorting does not simply migrate' is an assumption, not a fact, and then flags in the equity_impact field that 'middle-class exit to non-state or private schools' is 'the largest risk' that 'directly undercuts the assumption.' This is a critical internal contradiction. If middle-class families exit to non-state schools (church, foundation, private), sorting does migrate, and low-SES pupils left in general schools are harmed—not helped. The scenario frames this as an acceptable distributional trade-off ('within-disadvantaged distributional harm is possible even if aggregate gradient narrows'), but a conservative recognises this as a recipe for ghettoised general schools. (3) The implementation_planning flags 'acute teacher shortage in maths and science' as a 'binding constraint.' Removing selective schools while teacher supply is constrained is not reform; it is a guarantee of quality collapse. The scenario bundles teacher retraining, but the cost is flagged as 'high' and 'no unit cost available,' and the timeline assumes retraining begins before the pilot ('year 1-4'). This is not a realistic sequencing; it is a wish. Once retraining begins, sunk costs accumulate, and reversal becomes politically toxic even if the pilot fails—which is the Polish dynamic. (4) Faith-based and foundation schools are constitutionally entitled to their own educational mission and admissions criteria. Phasing out their 6/8-year tracks via legislative mandate removes a constitutionally protected choice. The scenario treats non-state maintainers as obstacles to be negotiated rather than as autonomous institutions. (5) The scenario acknowledges 'highest-intensity opposition from urban middle-class parents and prestige schools' and proposes a fallback of a 'reversible, staged pilot' if the cross-party pact fails. This is an admission that the reform lacks democratic consent. A conservative responds: if you cannot build a cross-party pact for structural reform, do not attempt it via pilot expansion and hope the sunk costs lock in successor governments. That is not reform; it is coup-like tactics.
reactions.condition_to_change: We could not support S3 under any plausible conditions the scenario outlines. However, we could conditionally support a very limited, time-bound pilot (maximum 5 years, maximum 2-3 districts) if: (a) it is explicitly voluntary for families and schools, with no closure of selective tracks; (b) non-state maintainers are explicitly exempted from the pilot requirement; (c) a parallel selective track (6/8-year gimnázium) continues in the same general-school buildings, so families retain choice; (d) the pilot includes a rigorous randomised control design with a comparison cohort, measuring peer-composition, teacher satisfaction, high-achiever retention (to schools, not just cohort), and SES-outcome gradient, with pre-specified thresholds for continuation; (e) teacher retraining is fully completed and uptake-monitored before the pilot begins, and no student cohort enters a de-tracked classroom until retraining is verified; (f) the pilot has an explicit fallback: if after 3 years peer-composition does not improve, SES gradient does not weaken, or middle-class exit exceeds 15%, the pilot is terminated and selective schools are reopened; and (g) a legislative supermajority or referendum is required for national rollout, not a mere Ministry decision after pilot completion. These conditions are so stringent that S3 is effectively not a policy we can support; they define a conservative's preconditions for even tolerating a pilot.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Conservative preference for maintaining institutional structures (selective schools) while addressing equity through resource distribution rather than structural change; support for rigorous standards and merit-based outcomes; but deep skepticism of whether compensatory funding can offset sorting without eroding the selectivity that makes selective schools effective.
reactions.interest: Selective schools and their families; merit-based achievement systems; taxpayers concerned about unsustainable public education spending; parents opposing structural upheaval.
reactions.public_good_frame: A society can improve equity in disadvantaged schools without dismantling selective schools by raising standards across the system and targeting resources to where they are most needed. Portugal's non-structural approach achieved PISA gains without early selection—though Portugal had no age-10 tracking to begin with. Hungary's selective schools produce high academic outcomes for their students; preserving that model while lifting disadvantaged general schools respects institutional autonomy and incentivises high performance everywhere. The conservative frame is: better standards and targeted support, not levelling-down through structural change.
reactions.argument: S4 is the most conservative-compatible scenario, but it contains a critical internal tension that we cannot ignore. On the merit: (1) Retaining selective schools respects institutional autonomy, parental choice, and the principle that schools should be free to pursue their academic mission. (2) Rigorous national curriculum standards and reduced grade retention are sound policies independently justified. (3) Targeted funding for disadvantaged schools is consistent with subsidiarity and efficiency—resources should flow to where need is greatest. However, S4 has two fatal weaknesses that make us conditional rather than supportive: (1) The scenario acknowledges in its own uncertainties that 'no country has demonstrated offsetting [compensation against early sorting] at Hungary's selection age.' This is the pivotal admission. S4 assumes compensation can offset sorting, but the evidence base does not support this claim. If compensation merely masks sorting rather than offsetting it, the reform entrenches the sorting mechanism while providing a fig-leaf that legitimises early selection. This is worse than honest sorting; it is sorting concealed. (2) Budget vulnerability is catastrophic. The scenario's political_risks field explicitly states: 'compensation lines are the first cut in fiscal consolidation, so gains are reversible across fiscal cycles.' This means S4 is not a stable reform; it is a temporary redistribution that disappears in the next fiscal crisis. Once a government suspends compensation to balance a budget, low-SES pupils in general schools are left with neither the compensation nor the selective alternative. The scenario flags this as a 'serious gap,' yet still proposes the reform. This is incoherent policy design. A conservative cannot support a scheme that is both untested and fiscally unstable.
reactions.condition_to_change: We could support S4 as a multi-year package if: (a) the compensation budget is explicitly ring-fenced in legislation via a dedicated revenue source (e.g., a small education-funding levy or maintenence tax on selective-school admissions fees, if non-state schools are required to charge uniform fees by family income) so that compensation is not vulnerable to annual fiscal cycles—the commitment must be credible and durable; (b) the package includes a rigorous pilot (2-3 disadvantaged school clusters with a comparison group) measuring SES-outcome gradient, peer composition, and teacher satisfaction over 5 years, with explicit pre-specified go/no-go thresholds for continuation (e.g., if SES gradient does not narrow by 0.1 SD within 5 years, the package is reformed or terminated); (c) non-state maintainers are required by law to follow the same curriculum standards and grade-retention rules as state schools, so that sorting does not migrate to unregulated schools as standards tighten; (d) teacher premia for disadvantaged schools are permanent and indexed to general salary growth, not discretionary, and a workforce audit demonstrates that supply can actually sustain the package; and (e) the scenario explicitly acknowledges that if compensation only masks sorting rather than offsetting it, the reform risks entrenching early selection permanently, and a fallback mechanism (e.g., moving to S1 admission reform) is legislated if the gradient does not improve within a decade. Under these conditions, S4 is a defensible conservative position: preserve structure, raise standards, target resources, but test ruthlessly and abandon if it becomes legitimisation of sorting without offsetting gain.
